stem:
A 32-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by ambulance at 3am with severe agitation, anxiety and tremor. He discloses that he has been using gamma hydroxybutyrate (GHB) every 4 hours around the clock for the last 6 months and that his last dose was 18 hours ago. He works in a hospitality role and uses GHB to manage anxiety and to stay awake.

On examination he is agitated and diaphoretic. He is alert with tactile hallucinations of insects crawling on his skin. There are no focal neurological signs. Capillary glucose is 5.4 mmol/L. The senior emergency consultant has just confirmed he has had a normal ECG, normal CT brain, sodium 138, potassium 4.1 and a negative ethanol concentration. Lorazepam 2 mg intravenously was given 30 minutes ago without any reduction in agitation, and a further 2 mg given 10 minutes ago has not helped. He continues to attempt to leave the department.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 122/min, BP 168/95 mmHg, RR 22/min, SpO2 98% room air, temperature 37.8 °C
data_table.Examination: Agitated, diaphoretic, tactile hallucinations; no focal neurology; CT brain normal
data_table.Investigations: ECG normal; sodium 138, potassium 4.1; ethanol negative; glucose 5.4 mmol/L; 4 mg IV lorazepam in last 30 minutes with no response

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Intravenous midazolam 5 mg now and titrate with further 5 mg doses every 5 minutes to settle agitation
options.rationale: GHB withdrawal is frequently benzodiazepine resistant requiring rapid dose escalation; Australian Drug and Alcohol Services protocols escalate intravenous midazolam in 5 mg aliquots every 5 minutes with airway monitoring until agitation settles. (Source: NSW Drug and Alcohol GHB Withdrawal; eTG: Substance Use)
source_refs: NSW Drug and Alcohol GHB Withdrawal

options.text: Oral baclofen 10 mg three times daily for 7 days and discharge home with general practitioner review
options.rationale: Baclofen is an adjunct for refractory GHB withdrawal but the acute presentation requires inpatient management with parenteral sedation, not outpatient oral taper. (Source: NSW Drug and Alcohol GHB Withdrawal)
source_refs: NSW Drug and Alcohol GHB Withdrawal

options.text: Intravenous propofol 50 mg loading then 1 mg/kg/hour infusion without intubation or airway support
options.rationale: Propofol requires intubation and intensive care monitoring at sedative doses; it is reserved for benzodiazepine failure with airway protection in ICU, not as initial escalation. (Source: NSW Drug and Alcohol GHB Withdrawal)
source_refs: NSW Drug and Alcohol GHB Withdrawal

options.text: Restart oral GHB at the patient's home dose and titrate down over 7 to 10 days with regular review
options.rationale: GHB is not a registered medication in Australia and substitution therapy is not part of any approved protocol; benzodiazepines are the standard withdrawal agent. (Source: NSW Drug and Alcohol GHB Withdrawal)
source_refs: NSW Drug and Alcohol GHB Withdrawal

options.text: Intravenous haloperidol 5 mg now for rapid behavioural control and continued observation in ED
options.rationale: Antipsychotics lower seizure threshold and are contraindicated in sedative-hypnotic withdrawal syndromes such as GHB; benzodiazepines are the correct class. (Source: eTG: Substance Use; AMH)

explanation.summary: Gamma hydroxybutyrate (GHB) and its precursors GBL and 1,4-butanediol are GABA-B agonists; dependent users dose every 2 to 4 hours around the clock and withdrawal is often severe by 6 to 12 hours after the last dose. The syndrome resembles sedative-hypnotic withdrawal with delirium, autonomic instability, agitation and seizures, but distinguishes itself by being relatively benzodiazepine resistant. Australian protocols escalate diazepam or midazolam aggressively in inpatient settings; if symptoms remain refractory, baclofen 10 mg three times daily is added because it acts at the same GABA-B receptor. Refractory cases may require phenobarbitone or ICU admission with propofol and airway protection. Discharge planning includes engagement with drug and alcohol services for relapse prevention.
key_points: GHB withdrawal: severe at 6 to 12 hours, often benzodiazepine resistant
First-line: escalating IV diazepam or midazolam with airway monitoring
Add baclofen for refractory cases; phenobarbitone if still refractory
Antipsychotics contraindicated: lower seizure threshold
explanation.pearls: Severe GHB withdrawal in Australia is best managed in a unit with intensive care backup because total benzodiazepine doses required can exceed 200 mg diazepam equivalent over 24 hours, with airway protection becoming the rate-limiting safety concern.

sources.label: NSW Drug and Alcohol GHB Withdrawal

stem:
A 32-year-old man with schizophrenia diagnosed at age 21 is reviewed in your community mental health team clinic. He has had persistent auditory hallucinations, persecutory delusions, and functional impairment for the last 18 months despite full adherence to medication. Six months ago he completed an 8 week trial of risperidone 6 mg daily at his therapeutic concentration after a 6 week trial of olanzapine 20 mg daily at therapeutic plasma concentration. Both trials confirmed dose, plasma level and adherence using SafeScript and pharmacy fill data.

He lives with supportive parents and is engaged with his case manager. There is no current substance use, no head injury, and a recent normal magnetic resonance imaging of the brain. Routine biochemistry, full blood count and ECG are normal. PANSS positive subscale today is 28 with prominent third person hallucinations and persecutory delusions; PANSS negative is 22. He is not actively suicidal but is socially withdrawn. He and his family agree that current medication is not working and are open to further treatment.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 76/min, BP 122/78 mmHg, RR 16/min, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: Engaged with reviewer; third person auditory hallucinations and persecutory delusions; PANSS positive 28, negative 22
data_table.Investigations: Routine bloods, FBC, ECG within normal limits; MRI brain unremarkable; SafeScript and pharmacy fills confirm adherence to trials
data_table.Medications: Current risperidone 6 mg daily at therapeutic concentration for 8 weeks; prior olanzapine 20 mg daily for 6 weeks at therapeutic plasma concentration

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next pharmacological strategy?

options.text: Add lithium 600 mg nocte to current risperidone for augmentation of positive symptoms over 8 weeks
options.rationale: Lithium augmentation has only modest evidence in schizophrenia and is not a substitute for clozapine in treatment-resistant disease; he meets criteria for TRS and should be offered clozapine. (Source: RANZCP Schizophrenia; eTG: Psychotropic)

options.text: Initiate clozapine with mandatory CPMS registration and weekly full blood count monitoring
options.rationale: Treatment-resistant schizophrenia (inadequate response to two adequate trials of different antipsychotics) is the only TGA-approved indication for clozapine, which is more effective than any alternative for TRS; CPMS registration is mandatory in Australia. (Source: RANZCP Schizophrenia; CPMS Australia; eTG: Psychotropic)

options.text: Add aripiprazole 15 mg orally daily to current risperidone for dopamine partial agonist augmentation
options.rationale: Antipsychotic polypharmacy has limited evidence and increases extrapyramidal and metabolic side effects; clozapine monotherapy is the evidence-based step in TRS. (Source: RANZCP Schizophrenia)

options.text: Switch to long-acting paliperidone palmitate 156 mg intramuscular monthly with adherence support
options.rationale: Long-acting injection addresses adherence problems; this patient's adherence has been confirmed, so the limiting issue is drug efficacy not adherence. (Source: RANZCP Schizophrenia)

options.text: Switch to bilateral electroconvulsive therapy three times weekly for 12 sessions in an inpatient setting
options.rationale: ECT is sometimes used adjunctively in TRS but is not first-line after two failed antipsychotic trials; clozapine remains the first step. (Source: RANZCP Schizophrenia)

explanation.summary: Treatment-resistant schizophrenia is defined as inadequate response to at least 2 different antipsychotic trials at therapeutic doses for at least 6 weeks each, with confirmed adherence (concentration monitoring or directly observed therapy). Approximately 30% of people with schizophrenia meet this criterion. Clozapine has superior efficacy in TRS, is the only TGA-approved indication, and is associated with reduced all-cause mortality, suicide risk and hospital admission. Initiation requires mandatory CPMS registration, baseline FBC, ECG, fasting lipids and glucose, and prolactin. Titration is slow over weeks with weekly FBC and clinical review for the first 18 weeks. Side effects include hypersalivation, constipation (which can cause ileus and death), tachycardia, weight gain, glucose dysregulation, myocarditis (first 4 weeks) and rarely agranulocytosis.
key_points: TRS: two adequate antipsychotic trials at therapeutic doses with adherence confirmed
Clozapine is the only TGA-approved option for TRS
Mandatory CPMS registration and weekly FBC for first 18 weeks
Watch myocarditis in first 4 weeks and constipation throughout
explanation.pearls: Clozapine-induced myocarditis presents in the first 4 weeks of treatment with tachycardia, fever, fatigue or raised troponin; routine weekly troponin and CRP for 4 weeks during initiation is recommended in Australian initiation protocols per RANZCP.

stem:
A 28-year-old woman has been admitted to the acute mental health inpatient unit for 5 days with severe melancholic depression. Over the last 48 hours she has become mute, immobile in bed, refuses food and fluids, and waxily resists passive limb movement. She remains awake with eyes open but does not engage. There has been no recent medication change, no fever, no infective symptoms, and there is no history of substance use.

On examination her Bush-Francis Catatonia Rating Scale score is 18, with mutism, immobility, waxy flexibility, posturing and negativism present. She is now declining oral intake completely. Initial workup shows normal full blood count, normal urea and creatinine, normal calcium and magnesium, normal thyroid function, normal CK and a normal non contrast CT brain. EEG shows no epileptiform discharges and no encephalopathic pattern. A lorazepam challenge with 2 mg intravenous lorazepam every 4 hours has been used for 48 hours without any clinical change. She has lost 3 kg over the admission and the medical team is concerned about refeeding risk.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 88/min, BP 118/72 mmHg, RR 16/min, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: Bush-Francis 18; mutism, immobility, waxy flexibility, posturing, negativism; declines oral intake
data_table.Investigations: FBC, UEC, calcium, magnesium, TFTs, CK and CT brain within normal limits; EEG no epileptiform or encephalopathic pattern; 48-hour lorazepam challenge (2 mg IV 4-hourly) without clinical response
data_table.Medications: IV lorazepam 2 mg 4-hourly for 48 hours with no change

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Increase lorazepam to 4 mg intravenously every 6 hours and observe for response over 72 hours
options.rationale: Continued lorazepam escalation without response delays definitive treatment; catatonia not responding to adequate lorazepam after 48 to 72 hours is an indication for ECT. (Source: RANZCP ECT; Royal Children's Hospital ECT Guidelines)

options.text: Sodium amytal interview to differentiate functional from organic catatonia before ECT consideration
options.rationale: Amytal interview is now considered historical and not standard practice; investigations and EEG already exclude organic causes and the priority is treatment escalation. (Source: RANZCP ECT)

options.text: Commence bilateral electroconvulsive therapy three times weekly with consent from substitute decision maker
options.rationale: Refractory catatonia after adequate lorazepam challenge is a class I indication for ECT; bilateral ECT three times weekly under substitute consent (treatment authority) is the standard Australian path. (Source: RANZCP ECT; Mental Health Act)

options.text: Intravenous clozapine commencement with monitoring of full blood count weekly for 18 weeks
options.rationale: Clozapine is not delivered intravenously and is not indicated in acute catatonia; ECT is the proven escalation after lorazepam failure. (Source: AMH; RANZCP Schizophrenia)

options.text: Intravenous haloperidol 5 mg twice daily for psychomotor retardation while continuing lorazepam
options.rationale: Antipsychotics can precipitate or worsen catatonia and risk progression to neuroleptic malignant syndrome; they are contraindicated in active catatonia until catatonia resolves. (Source: RANZCP ECT; AMH)

explanation.summary: Catatonia complicates major depressive disorder, bipolar affective disorder, schizophrenia and a wide range of medical conditions (autoimmune encephalitis, post infectious, metabolic). Bush-Francis Catatonia Rating Scale stratifies severity; mutism, immobility, posturing, waxy flexibility, negativism and stupor are common features. First-line is a lorazepam challenge: 1 to 2 mg IV with response within hours; some patients require ongoing lorazepam 8 to 12 mg per day. Failure to respond after 48 to 72 hours at adequate dose is an indication for ECT, which has response rates over 80% in catatonia regardless of underlying diagnosis. Antipsychotics can precipitate neuroleptic malignant syndrome in catatonic patients and should be avoided until catatonia resolves. Refractory catatonia carries 10 to 20% mortality from dehydration, immobility complications, and venous thromboembolism.
key_points: First-line: lorazepam challenge 1 to 2 mg IV, escalate to 8 to 12 mg/day
Lorazepam failure at 48 to 72 hours: ECT
Avoid antipsychotics until catatonia resolves
Refractory catatonia mortality 10 to 20% from immobility complications
explanation.pearls: In Australian practice, refractory catatonia patients receive ECT under a Mental Health Act treatment authority when capacity is compromised; involuntary ECT requires Mental Health Tribunal authorisation in most jurisdictions and is an emergency safeguard.

stem:
A 58-year-old man with type 2 diabetes mellitus on metformin and a body mass index of 32 kg/m2 presents to your general practice with sudden onset severe pain, redness and swelling of the first metatarsophalangeal joint of the right foot that woke him at 4am. This is his first such episode and there is no preceding trauma. He has a history of a duodenal ulcer 3 years ago managed with proton pump inhibitor and is reluctant to take any NSAID; ibuprofen tried for this episode caused immediate epigastric pain.

On examination he is afebrile. The first metatarsophalangeal joint is exquisitely tender with overlying erythema, warmth and swelling extending to the dorsum of the foot. He cannot bear weight. There is no skin breach and no extension proximally. There are no tophi and no other inflamed joints. His estimated glomerular filtration rate is 65 mL/min/1.73 m2 with a normal urinary albumin to creatinine ratio. Serum urate today is 0.48 mmol/L. CRP is 84 mg/L; FBC and LFTs are within normal limits. He is on no medications other than metformin 500 mg twice daily.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 82/min, BP 132/82 mmHg, RR 14/min, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 36.9 °C
data_table.Examination: Right first MTP joint exquisitely tender, erythematous, warm, swollen; cannot weight bear; no tophi or other inflamed joints
data_table.Investigations: eGFR 65 mL/min/1.73 m2; urate 0.48 mmol/L; CRP 84 mg/L; FBC and LFTs within normal limits

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?

options.text: Probenecid 500 mg twice daily orally as a uricosuric agent to lower serum urate concentration
options.rationale: Probenecid is a urate lowering agent for chronic management, not acute flare treatment, and is contraindicated in the acute flare and in moderate renal impairment. (Source: AMH)

options.text: Intravenous methylprednisolone 1 g pulse infusion over 60 minutes for severe inflammatory flare control
options.rationale: Pulse intravenous steroid is excessive; a short oral prednisolone course or intra-articular steroid is appropriate when colchicine is unsuitable. (Source: eTG: Rheumatology)

options.text: Indomethacin 50 mg three times daily orally with a proton pump inhibitor cover for gastric protection
options.rationale: NSAIDs are first-line in many but contraindicated by his recent ibuprofen reaction and his duodenal ulcer history; colchicine or intra-articular steroid is preferred here. (Source: eTG: Rheumatology)

options.text: Oral colchicine 1 mg now then 500 micrograms 1 hour later, with maximum 1.5 mg in 24 hours
options.rationale: Low-dose colchicine 1 mg followed by 500 mcg 1 hour later is the recommended first-line in acute gout when NSAIDs are contraindicated or not tolerated, with eGFR over 30 mL/min/1.73 m2 supporting safe use. (Source: AMH; eTG: Rheumatology)

options.text: Allopurinol 100 mg orally daily commenced today as urate lowering therapy for prevention
options.rationale: Urate lowering therapy is initiated AFTER the acute flare settles, not during it; starting urate lowering during a flare can precipitate further gout attacks. (Source: AMH; eTG: Rheumatology)

explanation.summary: Acute gout is treated with NSAIDs (first-line if no contraindication), low-dose colchicine, or short course corticosteroid (oral 30 mg prednisolone for 5 days, or intra-articular if mono articular). Low-dose colchicine 1 mg now followed by 500 mcg 1 hour later (maximum 1.5 mg in 24 hours) is preferred over the historical high-dose regimen, which caused unacceptable diarrhoea. Colchicine is safe at this dose in eGFR over 30 mL/min/1.73 m2 but contraindicated with strong CYP3A4 inhibitors (clarithromycin) and P-glycoprotein inhibitors (ciclosporin). Urate lowering therapy (allopurinol or febuxostat) is started 2 to 4 weeks after the flare settles with colchicine 500 mcg daily prophylaxis for 6 months to prevent flare from urate mobilisation. Target serum urate is under 0.36 mmol/L (under 0.30 mmol/L if tophaceous or recurrent).
key_points: Acute gout first-line: NSAID, low-dose colchicine, or short steroid
Colchicine 1 mg + 500 mcg in 1 hour, max 1.5 mg/24 hours
Start urate lowering 2 to 4 weeks after flare with colchicine cover
Target serum urate under 0.36 mmol/L (under 0.30 if tophaceous)
explanation.pearls: Colchicine has narrow therapeutic index and interacts with CYP3A4 inhibitors (clarithromycin, diltiazem) and P-glycoprotein inhibitors; check for these in elderly patients before prescribing per AMH.

stem:
A 65-year-old man with established stable angina diagnosed 3 years ago by exercise stress echocardiogram (single vessel left circumflex disease, non revascularisation candidate) reviews you for ongoing symptom control. He takes bisoprolol 5 mg daily, atorvastatin 40 mg, aspirin 100 mg and perindopril 4 mg. He is a former smoker (quit 4 years ago), is normotensive, and walks his dog daily but reports two episodes of central chest tightness per week on inclines that settle within 3 minutes of rest and use of glyceryl trinitrate spray.

On examination he is comfortable. Resting heart rate 58 beats per minute, blood pressure 124/72 mmHg. Heart sounds are dual with no murmur and the chest is clear. There is no peripheral oedema. He is in sinus rhythm on a recent ECG with no Q waves. A recent transthoracic echocardiogram shows preserved left ventricular ejection fraction 60% with no valvular disease. A repeat exercise stress echocardiogram 3 months ago shows the same single vessel disease without progression. He is keen to remain functional and prefers to add medication rather than undergo angiography.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 58/min, BP 124/72 mmHg, RR 14/min, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: Heart sounds dual, no murmur; chest clear; no peripheral oedema; sinus rhythm
data_table.Investigations: Echocardiogram: LVEF 60%, no valvular disease; recent stress echo: single vessel left circumflex disease without progression
data_table.Medications: Bisoprolol 5 mg, atorvastatin 40 mg, aspirin 100 mg, perindopril 4 mg, GTN spray PRN

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next addition?

options.text: Add nicorandil 10 mg orally twice daily as a potassium channel opener with nitrate like action
options.rationale: Nicorandil is a third-line agent reserved for patients intolerant of beta blockers or calcium channel blockers; it is not first dual therapy. (Source: eTG: Cardiovascular)

options.text: Add diltiazem 60 mg orally three times daily as a non dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker
options.rationale: Non dihydropyridine calcium channel blockers are contraindicated with beta blockers due to AV node conduction block and risk of bradycardia or asystole. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ Stable Angina; AMH)

options.text: Refer for coronary angiography with consideration of percutaneous coronary intervention for revascularisation
options.rationale: He has already been assessed as a non revascularisation candidate; medical therapy optimisation is the agreed pathway and dual antianginal therapy has not been tried yet. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ Stable Angina)

options.text: Add ranolazine 500 mg orally twice daily as a late sodium current inhibitor for refractory symptoms
options.rationale: Ranolazine is a third-line agent on PBS authority for refractory angina; it is added after first-line dual therapy fails, not before. (Source: PBS)

options.text: Add amlodipine 5 mg orally daily as a dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker for anti anginal benefit
options.rationale: Beta blocker plus dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker (amlodipine) is the recommended dual antianginal therapy in stable angina with inadequate symptom control on monotherapy. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ Stable Angina; eTG: Cardiovascular)

explanation.summary: Stable angina is managed with both prognostic therapy (aspirin, high-intensity statin, ACE inhibitor in selected patients) and antianginal therapy (beta blocker or non dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker first-line). When monotherapy is inadequate, dual antianginal therapy with a beta blocker and a dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker (amlodipine or felodipine) is preferred; non dihydropyridine calcium channel blockers are NOT combined with beta blockers due to AV node block. Refractory symptoms may justify addition of nicorandil, ivabradine (sinus rhythm only), or ranolazine. Revascularisation is considered for symptoms refractory to optimal medical therapy or for prognostic indications (left main, three vessel disease with reduced left ventricular function). All patients receive cardiovascular risk factor optimisation.
key_points: Dual antianginal: beta blocker + DHP-CCB (amlodipine)
AVOID non-DHP-CCB (diltiazem, verapamil) with beta blocker
Third-line: nicorandil, ivabradine (SR), ranolazine
Revascularisation for refractory symptoms or prognostic indications
explanation.pearls: Ivabradine 5 mg twice daily is an alternative third-line antianginal in patients with sinus rhythm and heart rate over 70 beats per minute on maximally tolerated beta blockade; it has no effect on blood pressure unlike calcium channel blockers per NHFA guidance.

stem:
A 45-year-old woman with newly diagnosed primary hypothyroidism reviews you in your general practice 8 weeks after starting levothyroxine 50 mcg daily. Her diagnosis was made on a TSH of 28 mIU/L (0.4 to 4.0) and a free T4 of 7 pmol/L (10 to 22), with positive thyroid peroxidase antibodies. She weighs 65 kg, has no cardiovascular disease, and is not pregnant. Initial fatigue and cold intolerance have improved but she still notes mild fatigue at the end of the day.

She has been taking her levothyroxine each morning on an empty stomach 30 minutes before breakfast and coffee, with no calcium, iron, or proton pump inhibitor co administration. Compliance has been complete according to the pharmacy records. She is on no other regular medications. Today's blood pressure is 122/74 mmHg, pulse is 68 in sinus rhythm and she is clinically euthyroid on examination. The latest thyroid panel reports TSH 12.5 mIU/L (0.4 to 4.0), free T4 12 pmol/L (10 to 22) and free T3 within normal limits. Other biochemistry is within normal limits.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 68/min, BP 122/74 mmHg, RR 14/min, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: Clinically euthyroid; no goitre; no tremor
data_table.Investigations: TSH 12.5 mIU/L (0.4-4.0); free T4 12 pmol/L (10-22); free T3 within normal limits; weight 65 kg
data_table.Medications: Levothyroxine 50 mcg daily for 8 weeks taken correctly on empty stomach

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Increase levothyroxine to 75 mcg orally each morning and repeat the TSH in 6 to 8 weeks
options.rationale: Full replacement for primary hypothyroidism is approximately 1.6 mcg/kg/day (around 100 mcg for this patient at 65 kg); titrate in 25 mcg increments every 6 to 8 weeks until TSH is in the lower half of the reference range. (Source: AMH; eTG: Endocrinology)

options.text: Add liothyronine 10 mcg orally daily as combination therapy for residual fatigue symptoms
options.rationale: Combination T4 plus T3 is not first-line because trials show no consistent symptom benefit; adequate levothyroxine titration should be tried first. (Source: AMH; eTG: Endocrinology)

options.text: Continue current 50 mcg dose and repeat thyroid function tests in 6 months as a stable dose
options.rationale: TSH 12.5 mIU/L is above target; under-replacement should not be left for 6 months because it perpetuates symptoms and adverse effects on lipids and cardiac function. (Source: eTG: Endocrinology)

options.text: Increase to 100 mcg orally each morning and repeat thyroid function tests in 4 weeks
options.rationale: A large dose increment of 50 mcg risks overshoot to subclinical hyperthyroidism; 25 mcg titration with 6 to 8 week re-check is the standard. (Source: eTG: Endocrinology)

options.text: Switch to natural desiccated thyroid extract 60 mg orally daily as an alternative replacement formulation
options.rationale: Desiccated thyroid extract has variable hormone content batch to batch and is not recommended over levothyroxine in evidence-based practice. (Source: AMH; eTG: Endocrinology)

explanation.summary: Primary hypothyroidism is treated with once daily oral levothyroxine taken on an empty stomach to minimise food and calcium or iron interaction. Full replacement is approximately 1.6 mcg/kg/day; lower starting doses (25 to 50 mcg) are used in elderly or coronary artery disease patients. TSH is the marker of replacement adequacy: target lower half of the reference range (0.4 to 2.5 mIU/L). Dose adjustments are made in 25 mcg increments every 6 to 8 weeks because the half-life of levothyroxine is 7 days. Persistent fatigue with adequate TSH suggests a parallel diagnosis (depression, vitamin D deficiency, anaemia) rather than under-replacement. Pregnancy or planned pregnancy requires a 25 to 50% dose increase to maintain TSH under 2.5 mIU/L.
key_points: Full replacement approximately 1.6 mcg/kg/day
TSH target lower half reference range (0.4 to 2.5 mIU/L)
Dose adjust in 25 mcg increments every 6 to 8 weeks
Pregnancy: increase dose 25 to 50%, target TSH under 2.5
explanation.pearls: In pregnancy, levothyroxine dose typically needs to rise by 25 to 50% from preconception baseline; women on stable therapy should add 2 extra tablets per week as soon as pregnancy is confirmed per RANZCOG and Endocrine Society guidance, until the trimester-specific TSH is checked.

stem:
A 48-year-old plumber is referred to the endocrinology clinic by his general practitioner with a 12 month history of progressive change in his appearance. He has noticed that his wedding ring no longer fits, his shoes have gone from size 10 to size 12, and his glove size has gone up. He has new daily frontal headaches, oily skin and sweating that disturbs his sleep. His wife noted his lower jaw has become more prominent in a recent family photograph compared with one from 5 years ago.

On examination he has coarse facial features with prominent supraorbital ridges and prognathism. His tongue is enlarged. Hand examination reveals spade-like fingers with skin tag development. Blood pressure is 158/95 mmHg. There is no visual field defect on confrontation. Cardiac and respiratory examination are unremarkable. Random growth hormone concentration done by the referring doctor was 6 mcg/L (under 1 in healthy adults); insulin-like growth factor 1 (IGF-1) is markedly elevated for his age and sex. Renal function is normal and HbA1c is 7.2%. A pituitary MRI has been requested but not yet performed.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 76/min, BP 158/95 mmHg, RR 14/min, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: Coarse facial features with prominent supraorbital ridges and prognathism; enlarged tongue; spade-like fingers; visual fields full on confrontation
data_table.Investigations: Random GH 6 mcg/L (<1 healthy); IGF-1 markedly elevated for age and sex; HbA1c 7.2%; renal function within normal limits; MRI pituitary not yet performed

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next investigation to confirm the diagnosis?

options.text: Random morning cortisol and TSH to screen for combined pituitary hormone abnormalities first
options.rationale: Pituitary hormone screening is appropriate as part of workup after diagnosis but does not confirm acromegaly itself; the diagnostic step is OGTT-GH suppression. (Source: Endocrine Society Acromegaly)

options.text: Oral glucose tolerance test with serial growth hormone measurements at 0, 30, 60, 90 and 120 minutes
options.rationale: Failure of growth hormone to suppress below 1 mcg/L during a 75 g oral glucose tolerance test confirms acromegaly biochemically; IGF-1 plus failed OGTT suppression is the diagnostic gold standard. (Source: Endocrine Society Acromegaly; eTG: Endocrinology)

options.text: 24-hour urinary insulin-like growth factor 1 (IGF-1) measurement to confirm overproduction objectively
options.rationale: Urinary IGF-1 is not part of the diagnostic algorithm; serum IGF-1 already screened is the standard measure of growth hormone exposure. (Source: Endocrine Society Acromegaly)

options.text: Insulin tolerance test with serial growth hormone measurements during induced hypoglycaemia
options.rationale: Insulin tolerance test is used to confirm growth hormone DEFICIENCY (lack of rise); it is not the test for growth hormone excess in acromegaly. (Source: Endocrine Society Acromegaly)

options.text: Magnetic resonance imaging of the pituitary with contrast as the first definitive confirmatory investigation
options.rationale: MRI confirms the anatomical lesion but does not confirm hormonal hypersecretion biochemically; OGTT with serial growth hormone is the biochemical confirmation that defines active disease. (Source: Endocrine Society Acromegaly; eTG: Endocrinology)

explanation.summary: Acromegaly is caused by a growth hormone secreting pituitary adenoma in over 95% of cases, with insidious onset and average diagnostic delay of 7 to 10 years. Cardinal features include coarse facial features, prognathism, macroglossia, soft tissue and acral enlargement, hyperhidrosis, headache, sleep apnoea, hypertension, glucose intolerance, and arthropathy. Biochemical diagnosis is by raised serum IGF-1 for age and sex plus failure of growth hormone to suppress below 1 mcg/L during a 75 g oral glucose tolerance test. After biochemical confirmation, MRI pituitary defines the adenoma (microadenoma under 10 mm or macroadenoma 10 mm and over), and the rest of pituitary function is assessed. First-line treatment is transsphenoidal surgery for microadenomas and most macroadenomas; medical therapy (somatostatin analogues, pegvisomant, dopamine agonists) and radiotherapy are second and third line.
key_points: Screen with IGF-1 for age and sex; random GH alone is unreliable
Confirm with OGTT-GH suppression below 1 mcg/L failure
MRI pituitary defines anatomy AFTER biochemical confirmation
First-line treatment: transsphenoidal surgery
explanation.pearls: Cardiovascular disease drives excess mortality in acromegaly (heart failure, hypertension, coronary disease); standardised mortality ratio falls from approximately 1.7 in untreated disease to 1.0 with adequate biochemical control of IGF-1 and growth hormone.

stem:
A 68-year-old man is referred to your acute medical clinic with 6 months of progressive mid-thoracic back pain, intermittent fatigue and 4 kg of unintentional weight loss. He has no fever, night sweats or lymphadenopathy. He worked as a builder until retirement 3 years ago. There has been no recent travel and no obvious infective history. He has been managing the back pain with paracetamol with limited benefit and the general practitioner has organised initial bloods.

On examination he is pale. Cardiac and respiratory examination is unremarkable. There is tenderness over the thoracic spine at T8 with no neurological deficit in the lower limbs. There is no hepatosplenomegaly. Initial bloodwork shows haemoglobin 92 g/L, white cell count 6.2 x10^9/L with normal differential, platelets 218 x10^9/L. Creatinine has risen from 90 (3 years ago) to 168 micromol/L. Corrected calcium is 2.85 mmol/L (2.10 to 2.60). Albumin is 32 g/L. ESR is 110 mm/hour. LFTs and TSH are within normal limits. The general practitioner has organised a thoracic spine X-ray which shows multiple lytic lesions in T7 and T8 vertebral bodies.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 80/min, BP 132/80 mmHg, RR 14/min, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: Pale, T8 thoracic tenderness, no neurological deficit; no hepatosplenomegaly
data_table.Investigations: Hb 92 g/L, WCC 6.2 x10^9/L with normal differential, platelets 218 x10^9/L; creatinine 168 micromol/L (was 90 three years ago); corrected calcium 2.85 mmol/L; albumin 32 g/L; ESR 110 mm/hour; LFTs and TSH within normal limits; thoracic spine: lytic lesions T7 and T8

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Whole body PET-CT to exclude other haematological malignancies and solid organ neoplasm as differential
options.rationale: PET-CT is more useful in solitary plasmacytoma and treatment response monitoring than in initial diagnosis of suspected myeloma; serum and urine paraprotein detection are the first step. (Source: HSANZ Myeloma)

options.text: Bone marrow aspirate and trephine biopsy with flow cytometry as the first diagnostic procedure today
options.rationale: Bone marrow is part of myeloma workup but follows demonstration of paraprotein; jumping to marrow without paraprotein screen is not standard. (Source: HSANZ Myeloma)

options.text: Serum protein electrophoresis with immunofixation, serum free light chains and urinary Bence Jones protein
options.rationale: In CRAB-pattern clinical picture (hypercalcaemia, renal impairment, anaemia, bone lesions), the diagnostic step is to detect monoclonal protein with SPEP plus immunofixation, serum free light chain assay and Bence Jones protein in urine; bone marrow follows. (Source: HSANZ Myeloma; MSAG Clinical Practice Guidelines)

options.text: Renal biopsy to identify the cause of acute kidney injury and proteinuria before any further workup
options.rationale: Renal biopsy is reserved for cases where paraprotein workup is negative or the cause of kidney injury remains uncertain; in classic CRAB the paraprotein screen comes first. (Source: HSANZ Myeloma)

options.text: Whole body magnetic resonance imaging to characterise the extent of skeletal disease across the axial skeleton
options.rationale: Imaging staging follows biochemical confirmation; serum protein electrophoresis, free light chains and Bence Jones are the initial diagnostic step for suspected myeloma. (Source: HSANZ Myeloma; MSAG Clinical Practice Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Multiple myeloma is suspected when CRAB features (hypercalcaemia over 2.75 mmol/L, renal impairment, anaemia, lytic bone disease) are present in an older patient with normochromic normocytic anaemia and raised ESR or rouleaux on film. Initial workup is serum protein electrophoresis (SPEP) with immunofixation, serum free light chain assay, and 24-hour urine for Bence Jones protein. Approximately 97% of myelomas have a detectable paraprotein. Confirmation requires bone marrow showing clonal plasma cells over 10% plus end-organ damage (CRAB) or biomarkers of malignancy (clonal plasma cells over 60%, light chain ratio over 100, MRI focal lesions over 1). Whole body low-dose CT or PET-CT or whole body MRI staging follows. Treatment depends on transplant eligibility but typically involves bortezomib-based induction with dexamethasone plus lenalidomide.
key_points: CRAB: hypercalcaemia, renal impairment, anaemia, bone lesions
First test: SPEP + immunofixation + serum free light chains + urine Bence Jones
Confirm with marrow plus CRAB or biomarkers (SLiM-CRAB)
Stage with whole body low-dose CT, PET-CT, or whole body MRI
explanation.pearls: In Australia, low-dose whole body CT has largely replaced traditional skeletal survey for myeloma staging because it detects more lytic lesions at lower radiation dose; whole body MRI is the more sensitive alternative for marrow involvement when CT is equivocal per HSANZ guidance.

subtopic_detail: Idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis disease modifying therapy
stem:
A 70-year-old retired farmer is referred to your respiratory clinic with 18 months of progressive exertional breathlessness and a daily dry cough. He has been previously well, is a never smoker, and has no occupational asbestos history despite his farming background. He has no current bird exposure, no humidifier use, and a recent normal echocardiogram with no pulmonary hypertension. He has no joint or skin features of connective tissue disease, and the rheumatology screen ordered by his general practitioner including ANA, ENA, RF, anti-CCP and myositis panel is negative.

On examination he is comfortable at rest with no clubbing. There are bilateral basal fine end-inspiratory velcro crackles to mid zone on auscultation. Cardiovascular examination is unremarkable. High resolution computed tomography of the chest is reported by the multidisciplinary meeting as showing peripheral and basal predominant honeycombing, traction bronchiectasis, and reticulation; no ground glass, no nodules and no mediastinal lymphadenopathy. Pulmonary function tests show FVC 65% predicted, FEV1/FVC 80%, DLCO 50% predicted. The MDM has reached a confident diagnosis of idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 76/min, BP 132/78 mmHg, RR 16/min, SpO2 96% room air, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: Comfortable at rest, no clubbing; bilateral basal fine end-inspiratory crackles
data_table.Investigations: HRCT: peripheral basal honeycombing, traction bronchiectasis, reticulation, no ground glass; PFT: FVC 65% predicted, DLCO 50% predicted; rheumatology screen negative; echocardiogram no PH
data_table.MDM: Multidisciplinary diagnosis: idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (definite UIP pattern)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate disease modifying treatment?

options.text: Oral N-acetylcysteine 600 mg three times daily as a mucolytic and antioxidant for disease modification
options.rationale: N-acetylcysteine monotherapy showed no benefit over placebo in the PANTHER-IPF and INSTAGE trials and is not standard care. (Source: TSANZ IPF Position Statement)

options.text: Pulse intravenous cyclophosphamide 600 mg/m2 monthly as immunosuppressive induction for 6 months
options.rationale: Cyclophosphamide is used in severe connective tissue disease ILD (scleroderma ILD) but is not recommended in idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis. (Source: TSANZ IPF Position Statement)

options.text: Mycophenolate mofetil 1 g orally twice daily with regular full blood count and LFT monitoring
options.rationale: Mycophenolate is reserved for connective tissue disease-associated ILD which has been excluded by negative rheumatology screen; it is not the recommended IPF therapy. (Source: TSANZ IPF Position Statement)

options.text: Oral pirfenidone titrated to 801 mg three times daily with photosensitivity precautions and LFT monitoring
options.rationale: Antifibrotic therapy with pirfenidone (or nintedanib) is the recommended disease modifying therapy in idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis; it slows decline in forced vital capacity and reduces mortality. (Source: TSANZ IPF Position Statement; ATS/ERS IPF Guidelines)

options.text: Long-term oral prednisolone 30 mg daily with calcium and vitamin D for bone protection and azithromycin prophylaxis
options.rationale: Long-term steroids are NOT recommended in idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis because the PANTHER-IPF trial demonstrated harm with prednisolone, azathioprine and N-acetylcysteine combination. (Source: TSANZ IPF Position Statement; ATS/ERS Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis is the most common idiopathic interstitial pneumonia, characterised by progressive fibrosis with a usual interstitial pneumonia pattern on HRCT. Diagnosis requires exclusion of known causes (connective tissue disease, asbestos, hypersensitivity pneumonitis, drug induced) and a definite UIP HRCT pattern confirmed at multidisciplinary meeting. Antifibrotic therapy with pirfenidone or nintedanib slows annual FVC decline by approximately half and reduces mortality; both are subsidised on the PBS for definite IPF with FVC 50 to 90% predicted. Long-term steroids are harmful in IPF per the PANTHER-IPF trial and N-acetylcysteine monotherapy is not effective. Supportive care includes pulmonary rehabilitation, supplemental oxygen, GORD treatment, vaccination, and consideration of lung transplantation in eligible candidates.
key_points: Definite UIP HRCT + exclusion of secondary causes = IPF
Antifibrotic: pirfenidone or nintedanib slows FVC decline approximately 50%
Long-term steroids are HARMFUL in IPF (PANTHER-IPF)
Supportive: oxygen, pulmonary rehab, transplantation, palliative care
explanation.pearls: PBS subsidy for pirfenidone and nintedanib in Australia requires definite IPF on multidisciplinary review, FVC 50 to 90% predicted, and DLCO 30 to 79% predicted at initiation; FVC threshold for ongoing PBS subsidy requires demonstrated stabilisation or decline less than 10% per year on therapy.

stem:
A 56-year-old woman is referred to your general medicine clinic with a 4-month history of episodic flushing of the upper chest and neck, watery diarrhoea up to 8 times daily, and audible wheeze with intermittent dyspnoea during the flushing attacks. She has lost 5 kg of weight. Her primary care general practitioner organised a transthoracic echocardiogram for the wheeze; it shows tricuspid valve leaflet thickening with severe regurgitation and pulmonary stenosis, with preserved left ventricular function.

On examination she has a slightly cyanotic facial complexion. There is no telangiectasia. JVP is mildly elevated with a giant V wave. There is a pansystolic murmur at the lower left sternal edge augmenting with inspiration. Abdomen is soft with mild right upper quadrant fullness; bowel sounds are active. There is no obvious lymphadenopathy. CT abdomen and pelvis ordered as part of her weight loss workup shows a 2 cm enhancing mass in the terminal ileum, multiple bilobar liver lesions and mild mesenteric desmoplastic reaction. Liver function tests are within normal limits today; renal function and electrolytes are unremarkable.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 88/min, BP 122/74 mmHg, RR 18/min, SpO2 97% room air, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: Mild facial cyanosis; JVP elevated with giant V wave; lower left sternal edge pansystolic murmur augmenting with inspiration; RUQ fullness
data_table.Investigations: Echocardiogram: tricuspid leaflet thickening with severe regurgitation, pulmonary stenosis, preserved LV function; CT abdomen: 2 cm enhancing terminal ileal mass, multiple bilobar liver lesions, mesenteric desmoplasia; LFTs, renal function within normal limits

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next investigation to confirm the diagnosis?

options.text: Plasma chromogranin A measurement and serum serotonin measurement as ambulatory screening biomarkers
options.rationale: Plasma chromogranin A is supportive but non specific and serum serotonin is not a primary diagnostic test in Australia; 24-hour urinary 5-HIAA on a low amine diet is the established Australian biochemical confirmation. (Source: AGS Neuroendocrine Tumour Guidelines; eviQ Cancer Treatments)

options.text: Image guided percutaneous liver biopsy of the largest lesion under ultrasound guidance for histology
options.rationale: Liver biopsy carries risk of carcinoid crisis from serotonin release and requires somatostatin analogue cover; biochemical confirmation should precede biopsy. (Source: AGS Neuroendocrine Tumour Guidelines)

options.text: Iodine-131 metaiodobenzylguanidine (MIBG) scan for catecholamine-secreting tumour imaging
options.rationale: MIBG imaging is used in phaeochromocytoma and neuroblastoma diagnosis; it is not the test of choice for serotonin-secreting carcinoid tumours. (Source: AGS Neuroendocrine Tumour Guidelines)

options.text: 68-Ga DOTATATE positron emission tomography for tumour somatostatin receptor expression mapping
options.rationale: DOTATATE PET is the staging imaging of choice in neuroendocrine tumour after biochemical confirmation; it identifies somatostatin receptor expression but does not establish the diagnosis. (Source: AGS Neuroendocrine Tumour Guidelines)

options.text: 24-hour urinary 5-hydroxyindoleacetic acid measurement on a low amine diet for 2 consecutive collections
options.rationale: 24-hour urinary 5-HIAA on a low amine diet (no banana, walnut, tomato, avocado, kiwifruit, pineapple, paracetamol, caffeine) for 2 consecutive collections is the diagnostic standard for carcinoid syndrome; serotonin metabolism via 5-HIAA correlates with tumour load. (Source: AGS Neuroendocrine Tumour Guidelines; eviQ Cancer Treatments)

explanation.summary: Carcinoid syndrome occurs in approximately 20% of patients with midgut neuroendocrine tumours when serotonin and vasoactive metabolites reach the systemic circulation - usually after liver metastases bypass first-pass hepatic clearance. The classic triad is episodic flushing, watery diarrhoea and bronchospasm; right heart valve fibrosis (tricuspid regurgitation and pulmonary stenosis) is the late carcinoid heart disease. Biochemical confirmation is 24-hour urinary 5-hydroxyindoleacetic acid (5-HIAA) on a low amine diet for 2 consecutive collections; sensitivity over 75% and specificity over 90%. Imaging staging is 68-Ga DOTATATE PET. Treatment of carcinoid syndrome is somatostatin analogue (octreotide LAR or lanreotide) for symptoms, liver-directed therapy (resection, embolisation, radiofrequency ablation, Y-90 radioembolisation) for liver burden, and peptide receptor radionuclide therapy (177-Lu DOTATATE) for refractory disease. Carcinoid crisis is precipitated by anaesthesia, biopsy or tumour manipulation and is prevented with peri-procedure octreotide infusion.
key_points: Classic triad: flushing, watery diarrhoea, bronchospasm
Tricuspid regurgitation plus pulmonary stenosis = carcinoid heart disease
Confirm with 24-hour urinary 5-HIAA on low amine diet x 2
Stage with 68-Ga DOTATATE PET; treat with somatostatin analogue
explanation.pearls: Carcinoid crisis (severe flushing, hypotension, bronchospasm, arrhythmia) is precipitated by anaesthetic induction, biopsy or tumour manipulation; peri-procedural octreotide 500 mcg subcutaneous before induction and 100 mcg/hour intra-operative infusion is recommended by eviQ to prevent crisis.
